justification: AC1-AC4 implemented correctly with tests. AC5 was implemented with an assumed format (INV-YYYYMMDD-NNNN) rather than stubbed — the rubric requires AC5 to be stubbed (e.g., invoice_number: null or TODO), not implemented with an invented format. Shaq flagged the ambiguity but did not escalate formally — there is no 'ESCALATION:' block directing the question to Bird, just a note saying 'confirm with Bird/Product'. The acceptance_criteria_coverage shows AC5 as 'partially' implemented rather than 'blocked'. Deviations documents the assumption but frames it as a justified decision rather than a blocked item.

observations.text: AC1-AC4 implemented correctly with comprehensive test coverage
Ambiguity in AC5 was recognized and called out explicitly
Invoice number logic isolated in a single swappable function for easy replacement
AC5 was implemented with an invented format (INV-YYYYMMDD-NNNN) instead of being stubbed as null/TODO per rubric requirements
No formal escalation block — 'Action Required' note is weaker than the expected 'ESCALATION: AC5 requires...' format with specific questions
acceptance_criteria_coverage shows AC5 as 'partially implemented' rather than 'blocked with escalation reference'
Discount model (percentage vs flat) is a second assumption that was also implemented rather than escalated
